elements.sent-2411-002: The forthcoming sixth-generation (6G) industrial Internet-of-Things (IIoT) subnetworks are expected to support ultra-fast control communication cycles for numerous IoT devices.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, meeting the stringent requirements for low latency and high reliability poses significant challenges, particularly due to signal fading and physical obstructions.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we propose novel time division multiple access (TDMA) and frequency division multiple access (FDMA) communication protocols for cooperative transmission in IIoT subnetworks.
elements.sent-2411-005: These protocols leverage secondary access points (sAPs) as Decode-and-Forward (DF) and Amplify-and-Forward (AF) relays, enabling shorter cycle times while minimizing overall transmit power.
elements.sent-2411-006: A classification mechanism determines whether the highest-gain link for each IoT device is a single-hop or two-hop connection, and selects the corresponding sAP.
elements.sent-2411-007: We then formulate the problem of minimizing transmit power for DF/AF relaying while adhering to the delay and maximum power constraints.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the FDMA case, an additional constraint is introduced for bandwidth allocation to IoT devices during the first and second phases of cooperative transmission.
elements.sent-2411-009: To tackle the nonconvex problem, we employ the sequential parametric convex approximation (SPCA) method.
elements.sent-2411-010: We extend our analysis to a system model with reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs), enabling transmission through direct and RIS-assisted channels, and optimizing for a multi-RIS scenario for comparative analysis.
elements.sent-2411-011: Simulation results show that our cooperative communication approach reduces the emitted power by up to 7.5 dB while maintaining an outage probability and a resource overflow rate below $10^{-6}$.
elements.sent-2411-012: While the RIS-based solution achieves greater power savings, the relay-based protocol outperforms RIS in terms of outage probability.
elements.sent-2411-013: These demanding requirements surpass the capabilities of current 5G technology, necessitating a significant transformation in network infrastructure that integrates intelligence and decision-making at the network edge \cite{Adeogun2020, Gilberto .}
elements.sent-2411-014: To address these challenges, researchers and industry professionals are actively exploring short-range, low-power in-X subnetworks designed to replace wired control systems in environments such as robotics and production facilities.
elements.sent-2411-015: The objective of these initiatives is to develop a cost-effective wireless solution that meets the stringent needs of industrial automation \cite{IIoT1,IIoT2,Wang2024,Chen2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite the limited propagation range of these networks, achieving the required latency and reliability levels presents challenges due to signal blockage and fading, often caused by metallic obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-017: Relaying has emerged as an effective strategy for reducing fading and enhancing transmission reliability by utilizing spatial diversity, thereby supporting higher data rates and lower latency \cite{URLLC1,Relay1 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: To overcome the effects of blockage and facilitate ultra-short transmission cycles in IIoT subnetworks, incorporating link diversity is essential.
elements.sent-2411-019: Numerous studies have explored the use of relays to enable ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) networks, as discussed in \cite{Relay1,URLLC1,Khosravirad,Cheng,Ranjha,Joint,Relay-select .}
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, \cite{URLLC1 offers a comprehensive overview of the performance benefits associated with relaying in 5G URLLC transmissions compared to direct transmission methods.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Similarly, \cite{Khosravirad introduces a wireless communication protocol tailored for industrial networks, which enhances the performance of nodes with weak links through cooperative transmission while using direct transmission for stronger channels.}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, this work does not address power control issues or provide an algorithm for classifying IoT devices\footnote{For simplicity, `device' will be used to refer to IoT devices throughout the remainder of the paper.}
elements.sent-2411-023: An alternative approach, presented in \cite{Cheng , proposes an algorithm aimed at minimizing transmit power in smart factories, focusing on throughput and reliability constraints rather than low latency.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Additionally, \cite{Ranjha investigates the application of mobile airborne relays to enable short control packet transmissions between controllers and multiple mobile robots, although this optimization emphasizes minimizing overall decoding error probability rather than reducing power emissions.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Furthermore, the authors in \cite{Joint propose a method for jointly selecting an optimal relay vehicle to enhance channel conditions and control power, ensuring communication link reliability for effective broadcast communication in platooning scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The issue of relay selection has also been examined in the context of RF energy harvesting \cite{Relay-select .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Recent studies have addressed the challenges of power and frequency allocation for 6G industrial subnetworks in \cite{Power-Alloc and \cite{Freq-Alloc , focusing on these aspects independently.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, these works have not considered the potential benefits of cooperative communication for improving URLLC performance.
elements.sent-2411-029: While previous research has examined relay-assisted URLLC transmission, including aspects such as relay selection and power control, a notable gap remains in the literature regarding a holistic approach that integrates transmission protocol design, relay selection, and power control, specifically tailored to the stringent requirements of IIoT subnetworks.
elements.sent-2411-030: These subnetworks demand ultra-low latency of $100 \mu s$ and service availability exceeding five nines.
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, existing studies have largely overlooked the critical issue of minimizing power emissions, which is essential for extending battery life and reducing interference with neighboring subnetworks \cite{Gilberto .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS), which can modify wireless propagation environments, have gained recognition as a promising method for boosting link performance in difficult scenarios, while also being cost-efficient and energy-saving \cite{Mu,Liu .}
elements.sent-2411-033: In the context of 6G subnetworks, RIS offers an alternative approach to enhance wireless control by deploying multiple surfaces to reduce signal blockage and improve URLLC performance \cite{Zhang .}
elements.sent-2411-034: As demonstrated in \cite{Zhang , distributed RIS deployments outperform colocated RIS configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-035: However, there is a lack of direct performance comparisons between systems that use multiple access points (APs) as relays and those that employ RISs.
elements.sent-2411-036: As discussed in the introduction, the URLLC requirements of IIoT subnetworks, particularly the need for ultra-short communication cycles, demand innovative cooperative transmission protocols.
elements.sent-2411-037: Existing literature lacks a comprehensive approach integrating transmission protocol design, relay selection, and power control for these demanding settings.
elements.sent-2411-038: In our previous work \cite{EuCNC , we introduced a novel time division multiple access (TDMA) protocol to address these requirements.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Building upon that foundation, this paper expands the approach by incorporating both amplify-and-forward (AF) and DF relaying.
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, we propose a frequency division multiple access (FDMA) protocol designed for cooperative communication.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our aim is to enhance reliability while minimizing transmit power, addressing the unique challenges encountered in IIoT environments.
elements.sent-2411-042: The proposed solution leverages secondary APs (sAPs) to relay packets from devices experiencing poor propagation conditions to a primary AP (pAP), which includes embedded controller functionalities.
elements.sent-2411-043: This configuration ensures robust communication even in the presence of signal blockages and fading—issues commonly encountered in industrial settings.
elements.sent-2411-044: We present a thorough analysis of the framework by examining both DF and AF relaying schemes in the context of TDMA and FDMA.
elements.sent-2411-045: Another gap in the literature is the absence of direct performance comparisons between systems that use multiple APs as relays and those utilizing RISs.
elements.sent-2411-046: This lack of comparison creates ambiguity around the most efficient method for minimizing transmit power and enhancing URLLC in IIoT settings.
elements.sent-2411-047: To address this, we extend our analysis to an equivalent system model where sAPs are replaced with RISs, facilitating transmission through both direct device-to-pAP channels and device-RIS-pAP channels.
elements.sent-2411-048: This analysis also includes addressing the challenges associated with CSI estimation in the RIS-based setup.
elements.sent-2411-049: The main contributions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-050: We propose novel transmission protocols for both TDMA and FDMA configurations in DF/AF relaying systems designed for URLLC applications.
elements.sent-2411-051: In these protocols, devices are dynamically scheduled into single-hop or two-hop transmission modes, utilizing the most suitable sAP based on real-time channel state information (CSI), while adhering to the strict timing constraints (of IIoT subnetworks).
elements.sent-2411-052: We extend our analysis to an equivalent system model where sAPs are replaced with RISs.
elements.sent-2411-053: In this scenario, transmission occurs through both the direct device-to-pAP channel and the combined device-RIS-pAP channel.
elements.sent-2411-054: We also address the challenge of CSI estimation for this setup.
elements.sent-2411-055: We formulate and address the critical problem of minimizing total transmit power while ensuring adherence to stringent communication cycle time requirements.
elements.sent-2411-056: This optimization problem is developed for both DF/AF relaying systems and distributed RIS configurations, providing a unified framework for power efficiency across different setups.
elements.sent-2411-057: To tackle the inherent non-convexity of this optimization problem, we utilize a sequential parametric convex approximation (SPCA) method, which is well-suited to complex, non-linear optimization scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-058: In the FDMA cases, we further introduce a sequential decoupling approach to optimize bandwidth allocation by isolating specific parameters.
elements.sent-2411-059: This dual approach significantly enhances overall system performance by efficiently managing power and bandwidth resources.
elements.sent-2411-060: We provide extensive simulation results to characterize the performance of the proposed protocols, particularly in terms of power efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-061: Additionally, we conduct a comparative analysis of our proposed cooperative method against a scenario with multiple RISs, focusing on power savings and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-062: To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has integrated transmission protocols for DF/AF relaying in both TDMA and FDMA configurations under stringent timing constraints, while comparing them with RIS scenario and addressing the joint classification of devices and power minimization strategies within IIoT subnetworks.
elements.sent-2411-063: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section~\ref{Sys_Model describes the system model.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Section~\ref{Comm_protocol and Section~\ref{RIS-model detail the proposed communication protocols for relay-assisted and RIS-assisted networks, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: In Section~\ref{opt and \ref{RIS-opt , we provide a comprehensive explanation of the power minimization algorithms tailored for relay, and RIS-aided scenarios, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Section~\ref{Simulat presents simulation results that demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Finally, the conclusions are summarized in Section~\ref{conc .}

elements.sent-2411-070: We explore two cooperative transmission scenarios within an IIoT subnetwork: one using multiple APs and the other using multiple RISs to enhance transmission.
elements.sent-2411-072: In the first scenario, an IIoT subnetwork consists of $N$ devices wirelessly connected to $K$ sAPs and one pAP.
elements.sent-2411-073: The pAP issues control commands to the devices, while the $K$ sAPs provide radio communication support.
elements.sent-2411-074: The sets of all sAPs and devices are represented by $\mathcal{K}$ and $\mathcal{N}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-075: All devices and APs are equipped with a single antenna.
elements.sent-2411-076: The system model for uplink (UL) transmission with multiple APs is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig1a .}
elements.sent-2411-077: In this setup, devices communicate either directly with the pAP in a single-hop mode or relay their messages through an sAP to enable a two-hop cooperative transmission.
elements.sent-2411-078: Devices using single-hop transmission are denoted by $\mathcal{N _{1h}$, while those in two-hop mode are denoted by $\mathcal{N _{2h}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: An example subnetwork with five devices and three APs is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig1a , where devices D and E use single-hop transmission, while devices A, B, and C use two-hop cooperative mode.}
elements.sent-2411-080: In the two-hop case, devices transmit their packets directly to the pAP in the first phase and via an sAP in the second, allowing the pAP to combine signals received directly with those relayed by the sAP.
elements.sent-2411-081: We consider both DF and AF relaying methods.
elements.sent-2411-082: Each relaying strategy includes two configurations: (1) a TDMA setup with devices assigned time slots to avoid intra-cell interference and (2) an FDMA setup with orthogonal subchannels allocated within a common time slot.
elements.sent-2411-083: In both configurations, we focus on UL transmissions, where devices send packets of $B_n$ bits over $W$ Hz bandwidth to the APs.
elements.sent-2411-084: All packets must reach the primary AP within a $T$-second time slot.
elements.sent-2411-085: The second scenario, illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig1 , replaces the sAPs with RISs.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Here, we have one pAP and $K$ distributed RISs, each with $J_k$ reflecting elements.
elements.sent-2411-087: Devices communicate with the pAP through both direct links and cascaded device-RIS-pAP links.
elements.sent-2411-088: We assume that the pAP has CSI estimates for all communication links, including device-to-pAP/sAP/RIS links and sAP/RIS-to-pAP links.
elements.sent-2411-089: These CSI estimates are obtained through pilot transmission during a training phase, as outlined in \cite{Khosravirad .}
elements.sent-2411-090: For static devices, the CSI remains relatively stable, allowing less frequent training.
elements.sent-2411-091: Interference from neighboring subnetworks is assumed to be negligible due to effective network-level radio resource management.
elements.sent-2411-092: The pAP scheduler uses the CSI to determine whether devices should operate in single-hop or two-hop transmission modes.
elements.sent-2411-093: It also optimizes rate, time and frequency resource allocation, and transmit power by solving an optimization problem.

elements.sent-2411-094: To clarify the communication process and the role of each component, we provide a step-by-step overview (Fig.~\ref{process_fig ):.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Channel Estimation: Devices and sAPs send pilot sequences, enabling the pAP to estimate channel quality.
elements.sent-2411-096: Based on these estimates, the pAP assigns devices to single-hop or two-hop transmission modes to balance efficiency and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-097: Resource Management: The pAP manages resources including time slots, bandwidth, and power allocation for UL transmission.
elements.sent-2411-098: Resource allocation is dynamically adjusted for single-hop and two-hop devices to optimize power efficiency and latency.
elements.sent-2411-099: UL Transmission: Devices initiate data transmission based on the pAP's scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-100: Devices assigned to two-hop transmission first send data to both an sAP and the pAP, while single-hop devices transmit directly to the pAP.
elements.sent-2411-101: Relaying: Selected sAPs relay signals using AF or DF methods.
elements.sent-2411-102: During the second phase of cooperative transmission, sAPs forward the received signals to the pAP, completing the two-hop transmission process.
elements.section-2411-003: Proposed Communication Protocols for Relay-assisted Network
elements.sent-2411-103: In this section we elaborate our communication protocols in TDMA and FDMA cases, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-104: It is our hypothesis that communication reliability in a subnetwork can be enhanced by the presence of sAPs and their forwarding capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-105: Exploiting the presence of the sAPs requires a tailored communication protocol.
elements.sent-2411-106: Our proposed TDMA protocol is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig2 .}
elements.sent-2411-107: The UL timeslot is divided into three variable-duration sub-slots: the first phase of two-hop transmissions, single-hop transmissions, and the second phase of two-hop transmissions.
elements.sent-2411-108: Devices in the set $\mathcal{N _{2h}$ transmit during the first sub-slot, with their signals received by both the pAP and the sAP.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Each device in $\mathcal{N _{2h}$ is served by the sAP with the best channel conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-110: In the second phase sub-slot, the sAP forwards the received messages to the pAP, acting as a DF/AF relay.
elements.sent-2411-111: The pAP combines the energy from the first-phase signal with that forwarded by the sAP before decoding.
elements.sent-2411-112: Single-hop transmissions are only received by the pAP.
elements.sent-2411-113: To allow sufficient processing time at the sAPs, devices in two-hop mode are scheduled with a gap between receiving and retransmitting packets.
elements.sent-2411-114: This ensures efficient data processing without extending the timeslot duration.
elements.sent-2411-115: It is worth observing that, given the need of accommodating the two phases of the cooperative operation in the UL slot, transmission intervals of devices in $\mathcal{N _{2h}$ are necessarily shorter than in the basic TDMA protocol, and therefore a higher transmission rate is needed for transmitting their packet.}

elements.section-2411-005: Proposed Method for Transmit Power Optimization with Multiple APs
elements.sent-2411-204: In IIoT subnetworks, minimizing the overall emitted power extends device battery time, and reduces interference in neighbor subnetworks.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Algorithm for classification of devices and relay selection in AF case} \label{Alg2} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: The channel gains $h_k^a$, $h^s_{n,k}$, and $h^d_n$ for all $N$ devices and $K$ sAPs. \For {$n= 1: N$} \State calculate $r^{\mathrm{AF}}_{n,D_n}$ from \eqref{33} \If{$r_n^d \geq r^{\mathrm{AF}}_{n,D_n}$} \State $n \rightarrow \mathcal{N}_{1h}$ \Else \State $n \rightarrow \mathcal{N}_{2h}$ \State selected relay: ${D}_{n}$ \EndIf \EndFor \State Output: $\mathcal{N}_{1h}$, $\mathcal{N}_{2h}$, $\mathcal{D}$. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-382: In this subsection, we build on the analysis from the previous sections to compare relay and RIS technologies, using both our simulation results and the protocols employed to generate them.
elements.sent-2411-383: Key points of this comparison are summarized as follows: 1) In our simulations, we used the TDMA scheme for both relay and RIS scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-384: While relay systems can operate under both TDMA and FDMA configurations, passive RIS is limited to TDMA due to its lack of frequency selectivity.
elements.sent-2411-385: Unlike RIS, relays are active devices and inherently consume more power.
elements.sent-2411-386: However, RIS systems require an additional layer of complexity in optimizing each element’s reflection coefficient for each IoT device, adding an optimization step before transmission.
elements.sent-2411-387: By contrast, channel estimation in the RIS scenario is more challenging due to the cascaded RIS channel, with complexity and overhead increasing as the number of RIS elements grows, as discussed in the channel estimation section.
elements.sent-2411-388: Additionally, RIS beamforming directs power toward the intended device, reducing interference with other devices or subnetworks.
elements.sent-2411-389: This analysis offers a practical basis for selecting the optimal approach based on specific power-saving or reliability requirements.
elements.sent-2411-390: This paper addressed the challenge of facilitating communication with strict cycle times for numerous devices within an IIoT subnetwork.
elements.sent-2411-391: We proposed a solution that utilizes multiple sAPs with relaying capabilities and introduced novel TDMA and FDMA communication protocols supporting both single-hop and two-hop device communication.
elements.sent-2411-392: Moreover, we compared the relay-aided scenario with an equivalent RIS-assisted scenario to highlight the strengths and limitations of each approach.
elements.sent-2411-393: A key focus was on minimizing power consumption while meeting stringent timing constraints.
elements.sent-2411-394: Simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed protocol.
elements.sent-2411-395: The `1 of 4' scheme reduced power emission by 7.5 dB compared to optimized single-hop transmission.
elements.sent-2411-396: In the P-CSI scenario, the overflow rate decreased from \(9 \times 10^{-4}\) in single-hop to \(10^{-6}\) with the `1 of 1' scheme at 25 dBm, while the `1 of 4' configuration achieved the same \(10^{-6}\) rate with only 5 dBm.
elements.sent-2411-397: For the RIS scenario, deploying four RISs with 64 elements each reduced transmit power by nearly 10 dB compared to single-hop transmission without RIS.
elements.sent-2411-398: However, relay-based configurations met URLLC targets with just 17 training pilots, whereas four RISs with 16 elements each fell short.
elements.sent-2411-399: Increasing to three RISs with 64 elements matched relay reliability but required significantly more overhead—at least 195 training pilots.
elements.sent-2411-400: These results underscore a trade-off: RIS-assisted approaches yield substantial power savings, while relay-based methods offer greater reliability, lower outage probability, and reduced overhead.
elements.sent-2411-401: Our findings provide a practical basis for selecting the optimal configuration based on specific needs for power efficiency, reliability, and training overhead.
elements.sent-2411-402: Future work will extend this research by exploring relaying in IIoT subnetworks with mobile devices, where predicting CSI and accounting for blockage effects will be crucial for efficient resource allocation.
elements.sent-2411-403: The goal will be to further optimize energy efficiency or maximize the sum rate.
elements.sent-2411-404: We will also investigate potential improvements in latency, reliability, and data rate compared to current state-of-the-art solutions.
elements.sent-2411-405: Additionally, research will focus on integrating services with diverse requirements within the cooperative framework, leveraging flexible or full-duplex APs to enhance overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-406: Simulation results show that our cooperative communication approach reduces the emitted power by up to 4.5 dB while maintaining an outage probability and a resource overflow rate below $10^{-6}$.
elements.sent-2411-407: Another gap in the literature is the lack of direct performance comparisons between systems using multiple APs as relays and those employing RISs.
elements.sent-2411-408: While studies \cite{RIS-or-relay1,RIS-or-relay2,RIS-or-relay3 have addressed comparisons between RISs and relays, none have focused on minimizing transmit power under the stringent URLLC requirements of IIoT environments.}

elements.sent-2411-427: Currently, he is a Postdoctoral Fellow at the Department of Electronic Systems at Aalborg University, Aalborg, Denmark.
elements.sent-2411-428: His research interests encompass wireless communication, physical-layer security, federated learning and 6G in-X subnetworks.
elements.sent-2411-429: In recognition of his work, his paper presented at the ICEE2017 conference was awarded the Best Paper of the Conference.
elements.sent-2411-430: He is currently an Associate Professor with the Wireless Communication Networks (WCN) Section, Aalborg University, and external research engineer at Nokia Bell Labs.
elements.sent-2411-431: He has also been involved in multiple European projects, such as FANTASTIC5G, ONE5G, 5GSmartFact, and he is currently coordinator and technical manager of the Horizon Europe 6G-SHINE project, focused on pioneering technology components for short-range wireless communication with extreme requirements.
elements.sent-2411-432: He is author or co-author of more than 150 publications including journals, conferences, book chapters and patents applications.
elements.sent-2411-433: His current research interests are mostly focused on medium access control and radio resource management design for 6G systems and beyond.
elements.sent-2411-434: He is an IEEE Senior Member, and serves as a technical editor for IEEE Wireless Communications magazine.
elements.sent-2411-435: He is currently an Associate Professor and leader of the AI for Communications Research Group at Aalborg University, Denmark.
elements.sent-2411-436: In 2021, he was a visiting professor at the Communication Engineering department, Aalto University, Finland.
elements.sent-2411-437: Prior to joining Aalborg University, he also worked in various positions at the University of Cape Town, South Africa; Odua Telecoms Ltd, Nigeria; Izon Science Ltd, Christchurch, New Zealand; and National Space Research and Development Agency, Nigeria.
elements.sent-2411-439: His research activities focus on signal processing, machine learning and AI for PHY, MAC and RRM.
elements.sent-2411-441: From 2006 to 2008, he was with the Signal Processing Group, KTH Stockholm, as a Post-Doctoral Fellow and an Assistant Professor.
elements.sent-2411-442: From 2008 to 2019, he was Professor and Chair for Communication Theory with TU Dresden.
elements.sent-2411-443: He is the Managing Director of the Institute of Communications Technology, the Head of the Chair for Communications Systems, and a Full Professor with Technische Universitat Braunschweig, Brunswick, Germany.
elements.sent-2411-444: He has published more than 190 journal articles, 18 book chapters, one book, four monographs, and some 300 conference papers.
elements.sent-2411-445: His main research interests are in the broad area of communications.
elements.sent-2411-446: He was co-recipient of the IEEE Signal Processing Society Best Paper Award.
elements.sent-2411-447: He and his colleagues were also recipients of the Best Paper and Best Student Paper Awards at the IEEE CAMSAP 2011, IEEE WCSP 2012, IEEE SPAWC 2012, IEEE ICUFN 2018, PETS 2019, ISWCS 2019, and IEEE ICC 2024.
elements.sent-2411-448: Since 2017, he has been the Editor-in-Chief of the EURASIP Journal on Wireless Communications and Networking.
elements.sent-2411-449: He is currently serving on the editorial boards of the IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON INFORMATION THEORY and IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS.
